APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses predicate-argument structure analysis for Japanese using neural sequence and Grid-RNN models to capture multi-predicate interactions, a core NLP task suitable for top NLP venues. It targets the right audience (computational semantics/structured prediction) and positions itself against relevant prior work (Ouchi et al., Shibata et al., Zhou & Xu) while proposing a clear extension (multi-sequence/Grid-RNN with residual connections). The problem formulation, dataset (NAIST Text Corpus 1.5), baselines, evaluation (F-measure, bootstrap significance) and analyses (depth, per-case results) match expectations for a top-tier conference.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a recognizable structure (task → models → experiments) but the presentation is disrupted by numerous duplications, formatting artifacts and missing/only-referenced figures and tables (e.g., repeated paragraphs on pro-drop languages, stray "TeX output 2017.02.07:2041", references to Fig.3/Table1 without content). The main contribution is only loosely stated and not cleanly highlighted early, and several sentences and citations are duplicated verbatim which harms readability. Notation and equations are mostly present (GRU, h(ℓ)m,t, softmax), but inconsistent repetitions and editorial errors make it hard to follow important details.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments are substantial and appropriate: evaluation on the standard NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 with train/dev/test splits, comparisons to strong baselines (Imamura+09, Ouchi+15), per-case-role breakdowns (Table 3) and depth/residual analyses (Table 2) support the main claims (notably improvements on Zero arguments and with multi-sequence/Grid-RNN). Some ablations/sensitivity analyses are present (single- vs multi-sequence, with/without residuals, varying depth) but important ablations are missing (e.g., input feature ablation, hyperparameter sensitivity, effect of including syntactic features) and there is little discussion of failure cases or qualitative/error analysis beyond excluding Inter-Zero (noted as out of scope). Reproducibility is partially supported (dataset splits, model types, optimizer, implementation framework) but key details (hidden sizes, embeddings, training epochs, random seeds, exact hyperparameters) are not provided, so minor gaps remain.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—Japanese PAS and especially zero/omitted arguments in pro-drop languages—and removes dependence on syntactic parsers (focus on intra-sentential Dep/Zero arguments). It demonstrates practical impact by exceeding the state-of-the-art without syntactic information ("exceeds the accuracy of the state-of-the-art... without syntactic information") and reports sizeable gains (≈3 points for zero arguments and large improvements for DAT, ~30 points, as reported). Introducing a multi-sequence/Grid-RNN to model multi-predicate interactions directly from word sequences is likely to change practice and inspire follow-up work and cross-lingual application, so the impact is strong though not wholly transformative.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper adapts existing neural components (stacked Bi-RNNs/GRUs, residual connections) to Japanese PAS and, importantly, applies Grid-RNNs to model multi-predicate interactions (Sec. 4), which is a novel application within this task. The problem (Japanese PAS) is well-studied and many building blocks are standard; the authors clearly differentiate their work from prior neural SRL/PAS work (Sec. 5.3) and emphasize removing syntactic features while outperforming Ouchi et al. (2015) (Abstract/Experiments). Overall this is a meaningful but incremental methodological extension rather than a highly original breakthrough.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear claims (Grid-RNN multi-sequence and deep Bi-RNN models improve Japanese PAS without syntactic features) and supports them with experiments on the standard NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 using standard splits and appropriate baselines (Imamura et al. reimplementation and Ouchi et al. 2015). The training objective, model architectures (stacked Bi-GRU, Grid-RNN with residuals) and output softmax are specified and mathematically consistent, and significance testing via bootstrap resampling is mentioned. Minor shortcomings are missing low-level experimental details (exact numerical results/tables are referenced but not shown in the excerpt, hyperparameter/optimization specifics and reported p-values), but these do not undermine the presented evidence that the proposed models outperform the baselines.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against the standard pointwise baseline (Imamura et al., 2009) and the state-of-the-art joint model (Ouchi et al., 2015) and uses the same NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 splits and F-measure with bootstrap significance testing (Sect. 6.1–6.2). It acknowledges that other neural works (Shibata et al., 2016; Iida et al., 2016) used unpublished/different datasets so direct comparison is not possible (Sect. 5.3, footnote), and it provides ablation/analysis (network depth, residuals) and per-case-role breakdowns explaining where gains come from (Sect. 6.2). Overall the comparisons are fair, relevant, and the paper discusses prior methods and limitations appropriately.
